The Market House / Macro Sessions (Juliette Declercq, JDI Research)
AI Snake Oil (Arvind Narayanan & Akash Kapur)
WealthVest Macro (Drew & Tim, guest Manoj Pradhan)

skippedSources.name: Haymaker (***Premium Trading Alert***)
skippedSources.reason: Fully paywalled — email body contained only a title and unsubscribe link, zero sentences of preview content.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Analysts raised Q2 estimates mid-quarter for the first time since 2021
items.idea: JPMorgan's Market Intel desk says analysts have raised Q2 estimates 3.4% intra-quarter — the strongest upward revision cycle since Q2 2021, when estimates are normally cut as the quarter progresses. The bull case rests on macro stress not yet meeting micro (earnings) disappointment; that's the thing to watch break.
items.angle: Earnings season starting next week is the real test — if just one mega-cap misses despite this revision tailwind, the failure mode is violent because expectations are already priced for a beat, not a miss.
items.tweetA: Analysts raised Q2 estimates 3.4% mid-quarter — they normally get cut as the quarter goes on. Last time revisions went this direction: Q2 2021, right before a big run. JPM reads this as 'don't fade the rally.' I read it as 'there's zero room left for disappointment.'
items.tweetB: The bull case for stocks right now isn't multiple expansion — it's that Wall Street keeps raising the E in P/E mid-quarter, something that hasn't consistently happened since 2021. That's a floor until the first earnings miss. Then it's a trapdoor.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Semis are now crowded on both sides — longs AND shorts
items.idea: Ahead of earnings season, Energy and Health Care Equipment have flipped to consensus-long as bears get squeezed out. But Semis are crowded on both sides simultaneously — longs packed in, shorts building — making the sector what the piece calls 'the real pressure chamber.' China Sales Exposure remains one of the most crowded macro themes.
items.angle: Two-sided crowding in semis means whichever direction breaks first, it breaks hard and mechanically — that's a volatility setup into earnings, not a directional one.
items.tweetA: Semis are crowded long AND crowded short at the same time going into earnings. That's not a consensus trade, that's a keg of dynamite sitting under the whole tape. $SMH $NVDA
items.tweetB: Everyone assumes 'crowded' means one-sided. Hedge fund positioning in semis right now is crowded on both sides — which means the next print doesn't just move the stock, it liquidates someone's whole book either way.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: SK Hynix's $26.5B ADR was 7x oversubscribed — Wall Street wants AI hardware it can't get locally
items.idea: SK Hynix's planned $26.5B US ADR listing drew demand for more than 7x the 177.9M shares offered. Korean shares are already up roughly 700% over the past year. Kioxia is prepping a similar ADR after its own run in Japan — Asian AI-hardware names now want the dollar-trading, US-hours 'valuation halo.'
items.angle: This is a capital-plumbing story as much as an AI story — the marginal buyer for AI infrastructure exposure sits in New York, not Seoul or Tokyo, and that's about to pull more Asian hardware names west.
items.tweetA: SK Hynix: up 700% in a year in Seoul, and Wall Street still wants 7x more of it than it can get. When the local market runs out of AI-hardware supply, the ADR window becomes the new IPO pop. Kioxia is next. $MU
items.tweetB: Forget the China-ADR chill from the Didi era — memory scarcity just reopened the US listing pipe for Asian hardware. The read-through: any AI-adjacent Asian supplier now has to ask why it's still only listed at home.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Goldman's S&P 8,000 math isn't the debate anymore — the Fed path is
items.idea: Goldman has 2027 EPS at 385 vs consensus 398; split the difference at a 20.5x multiple and the index lands above 8,000, under 7% from current levels. The real fork: zero Fed hikes (consensus among equity accounts) vs ~2 hikes (priced by markets) vs 4+ hikes (what some macro accounts are positioned for). Momentum has drawn down 20%+ and positioning is 'cleaner' post-rinse.
items.angle: The index math is almost boring — the entire debate has moved into 'how many hikes,' which is a rates call disguised as an equity call.
items.tweetA: Goldman's S&P 8,000 target needs a 20.5x multiple on EPS that's basically already priced. The real question buried in the note: zero Fed hikes keeps this working, four-plus hikes breaks it. That's not an equity call, that's a rates call wearing an equity costume.
items.tweetB: Momentum factor down 20%+ and Goldman calls positioning 'cleaner.' Translation: the crowded trade got forcibly un-crowded, and now everyone's deciding whether to get back in before earnings season answers the AI-payoff question for them.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Micron's $250B US investment pledge did more for chip stocks today than falling oil did
items.idea: Chipmakers led the tape not because oil fell but because SK Hynix's 7x-oversubscribed ADR and Micron's plan to lift US investment to $250B gave the AI infrastructure story fresh validation. Oil separately eased as traders faded worst-case Iran scenarios. New model releases (GPT 5.6, Meta's Muse Spark 1.1, Grok 4.5) added narrative fuel.
items.angle: Two unrelated de-risking events — oil geopolitics and chip capex — got conflated into one 'risk-on' tape today. Worth separating, because they'll diverge again the next time Hormuz flares.
items.tweetA: Today's rally: oil calmed the room, but chips lit the fuse. Micron pledging $250B in US capex did more for semis than easing Iran fears did for the index. People keep treating this as one 'risk-on' story. It's two stories that happen to rhyme this week. $MU $SMH
items.tweetB: $250B is Micron's new US investment number. That's larger than the GDP of most countries, and the market barely blinked because it's now the baseline expectation for anyone in the memory/AI supply chain.

items.title: Wage growth just ticked up, and job-switchers are pulling further ahead
items.idea: The Atlanta Fed's wage tracker shows overall wage growth rose to 3.6% YoY in June from 3.5%. Job stayers went 3.3%→3.4%, but job switchers jumped 3.7%→4.1% — the switcher premium is widening, usually a tight-labor-market signal.
items.angle: A widening switcher premium keeps the 'sticky inflation' camp (see the Declercq item elsewhere in this window) in the driver's seat.
items.tweetA: Job switchers just went from a 0.4pt wage premium over stayers to a 0.7pt premium in one Atlanta Fed print. That's not a cooling labor market — that's one where quitting still pays. Good luck disinflating services with that under the hood.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching payrolls for the Fed's next move. The tell might be simpler: wage growth for job switchers just accelerated to 4.1% YoY while stayers are stuck near 3.4%. When switching pays a full point more than staying, workers move, and wages don't cool on their own.

charts.desc: Atlanta Fed wage growth tracker chart — job stayers vs switchers, both accelerating

items.title: Peak options fear, then an immediate reversal — the setup has a near-perfect track record
items.idea: The equity put/call ratio hit 0.86 — the most fearful options positioning in 4 months — then flipped as bulls retook control the same day. Historically, when put/call reaches this extreme, SPX has been higher 10 of 11 times five days later, median gain +1.2%.
items.angle: 10-of-11 is the kind of base rate that gets arbed away the moment everyone knows about it — a short-term tactical tailwind, not a structural one.
items.tweetA: Put/call ratio hit a 4-month fear extreme yesterday. History: SPX was higher 10 of 11 times, 5 days later, whenever it's gotten this stretched. Small sample, sure. But it's the kind of small sample that keeps working until the one time it doesn't.
items.tweetB: Sentiment did a full round trip in about 24 hours — peak options fear to bulls back in control. That whipsaw is itself informative: nobody has conviction right now, they're just reacting to headlines.

items.title: Sector correlations say bullishness is 'extremely high' — historically a warning, not a green light
items.idea: DataTrek's sector correlation measure shows bullishness at an extreme reading; historically that combination has preceded muted near-term gains and elevated vulnerability to macro shocks.
items.angle: This is the quiet contrarian counterweight to the 'earnings revisions are the best since 2021' bull case elsewhere in this window — both can't be fully right, and the market is currently choosing to believe the earnings story.
items.tweetA: One chart says positioning bullishness is as extreme as it gets. Another chart the same week says earnings revisions are the best since 2021. Both are true. The market is choosing to believe the second one. That's the whole risk in one sentence.
items.tweetB: 🔥 Sector correlation bullishness is at an extreme most people associate with tops, not with 'still cheap.' Worth screenshotting before everyone claims they saw it coming.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — sector correlation bullishness extreme, via @datatrekmb

items.title: US earnings revisions have been positive for the longest stretch since 2022
items.idea: Earnings revision momentum (net % of MSCI US names with rising vs falling year-ahead EPS forecasts) sits at 21.2% per Bloomberg Intelligence — the longest positive streak since 2022 and the highest level since 2021.
items.angle: A second, independent source corroborating JPMorgan's 3.4%-revision claim elsewhere in this window — two different desks, two different metrics, same conclusion: analysts have stopped cutting.
items.tweetA: Two different data providers, two different metrics, same story this week: JPMorgan says estimates are up 3.4% mid-quarter (rare). Bloomberg Intelligence says revision momentum is at 21.2%, longest positive streak since 2022. When multiple measures agree this hard, fade it at your peril.
items.tweetB: 21.2% net earnings revision momentum. The bear case has to explain why analysts, usually early to cut, keep raising numbers into an active shooting war in the Middle East. Either they're wrong, or the 'AI capex risk' story is overpriced relative to the actual P&L.

charts.desc: Earnings revision momentum chart via Bloomberg Intelligence

items.title: H100 rental prices are still rising, more than three years into the AI buildout
items.idea: Since July 2, the H100 GPU rental curve has risen again, with more providers hiking prices — compute market data (@silicon_data) shows continued tightening, not the softening you'd expect this deep into a capacity buildout.
items.angle: This directly cuts against the 'AI capex glut' bear thesis circulating elsewhere (Apollo/Slok-style AI-payoff-doubt pieces) — actual rental spot prices say demand still exceeds supply.
items.tweetA: H100 rental prices went up again this month. Three-plus years into the biggest capex buildout in tech history, and spot compute is still tightening, not softening. Whatever you think about the AI bubble debate, the physical market disagrees with the 'glut is coming' camp — for now.
items.tweetB: Bears keep pointing to hyperscaler depreciation schedules as the tell. Bulls should point to this: actual GPU rental rates rising, provider by provider, in real time. Follow the spot market, not the accounting.

charts.desc: H100 GPU rental curve chart via @silicon_data

items.title: Trump just let Ukraine build its own Patriot missiles — and turned NATO into a loyalty auction
items.idea: At the Ankara NATO summit, Trump reversed months of stalled air-defense access, telling Zelenskyy the US would let Ukraine manufacture Patriot interceptors domestically, while NATO displayed roughly $50B in new defense deals. The choke point shifts from 'does the US release stockpile' to 'can Ukraine stand up secure production before Russia hits the factories' — Russia's incentive is now to burn through Ukraine's interceptors before that capacity exists.
items.angle: This reframes the defense-industrial trade: it's not just 'more NATO spending,' it's a specific bet on Ukraine (and US contractors licensing IP) building missile production capacity under fire — a very different risk/reward than a straight order backlog.
items.tweetA: Trump didn't give Ukraine more Patriots. He gave them the license to BUILD Patriots. That's a completely different trade than 'NATO spends $50B' — it's a bet on whether Kyiv can stand up a secure missile factory before Russia finds it. Nobody's pricing that execution risk.
items.tweetB: Every NATO ally that shows purchases and Iran-alignment gets protected from Trump's trade threats. Every ally that resists gets Spain'd. That's not an alliance anymore — that's a loyalty auction with a $50B entry fee.

items.title: Hyperscaler and semiconductor free cash flow just staged a 'generational transfer'
items.idea: TME's chart of 12-month forward free cash flow for hyperscalers vs semiconductor companies shows a structural shift in where cash-generation power sits in the market — described as a 'generational transfer' rather than a cyclical rotation.
items.angle: If FCF generation is genuinely migrating structurally toward these two cohorts, that's the real justification (or refutation) for AI capex multiples — worth pulling the actual chart rather than trusting the one-line teaser.
items.tweetA: 🔥 'Generational transfer in free cash flow' — hyperscalers and semis, per TME's chart. If this is real and not a capex-cycle illusion, it's the best argument for paying up on AI infra names. If it reverses when capex slows, it's the best argument against. Open the chart.
items.tweetB: Free cash flow doesn't lie the way narrative does. Worth checking whether this 'generational transfer' holds up once hyperscaler capex growth decelerates — FCF looks amazing right now mostly because capex hasn't caught up to depreciation yet.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING — 12m forward FCF, hyperscalers vs semiconductor companies, $bn

items.source: AI Snake Oil (Arvind Narayanan & Akash Kapur)
items.title: The AI bubble debate is asking the wrong question — labs are escaping the commodity trap by locking in enterprises
items.idea: Narayanan and Kapur argue inference is structurally a commodity business ($4-8T of AI infra capex projected by the early 2030s needs to be recouped, but models are undifferentiated and switching costs are low) — yet leading labs are avoiding that trap not by winning the model war, but by moving 'up the stack' into higher-margin, stickier enterprise products that create lock-in.
items.angle: This reframes the entire AI-bubble-or-not debate: the real investable question isn't 'will inference margins compress' (they will), it's 'which labs successfully verticalize before that happens' — a narrower, more concentrated bet than 'own the AI theme.'
items.tweetA: Everyone's debating 'is AI a bubble.' Wrong question. Inference margins WILL compress to commodity levels — so which labs escape that by moving up the stack into locked-in enterprise products before it happens? That's a stock-picking question, not a macro one.
items.tweetB: $4-8 trillion in projected AI infra capex by the early 2030s needs to be recouped from a business (inference) that's structurally a commodity. The bull case was never 'inference stays high-margin' — it's 'labs verticalize into lock-in fast enough.' Anthropic's enterprise push only makes sense in this light.

items.title: Everything cyclical rallied except the thing that should've — energy, on a two-front oil war
items.idea: Nasdaq +1.3%, Russell 2000 +1.2%, PHLX Semis +3.1% on SK Hynix ADR excitement and easing yields/oil. Energy lagged even as the US struck Iran for a second day with Hormuz shipping concerns persisting — WTI still fell. Consumer Staples was the weakest sector (Costco soft comps, Pepsi miss). Existing home sales fell in June but median price hit an all-time high even as affordability improved to the best level since Aug 2025.
items.angle: Oil falling during an active two-day US-Iran strike exchange is the most counterintuitive data point of the day — the market has fully priced 'no Hormuz closure,' exactly the consensus assumption Apollo's Slok-style pieces keep warning is dangerous.
items.tweetA: US struck Iran for a SECOND straight day today and oil still fell. That's not confidence, that's complacency — the whole tape is one Hormuz headline away from repricing a 'de-risked' war that isn't actually de-risked.
items.tweetB: Home prices hit an all-time high in May even as sales volume fell and affordability supposedly improved to the best level since August 2025. Read that twice — 'best affordability in 10 months' and 'record prices' are describing the same housing market.

items.title: Bespoke's mid-year survey: pros now think the AI boom is in EARLIER innings than they thought six months ago
items.idea: Bespoke's follow-up to its mid-year market survey shows respondents shifting their view of the AI-cycle 'inning' earlier (more room to run) than six months prior — the piece promises additional indicators beyond sentiment, but the free preview cuts off before showing them.
items.angle: A survey where professional respondents get MORE bullish on how early we are, mid-cycle, is itself a sentiment data point worth tracking against the two-sided semis crowding above — sentiment and positioning are starting to diverge.
items.tweetA: Bespoke's survey: pros now think we're in an EARLIER inning of the AI boom than they thought six months ago. Meanwhile hedge fund positioning in semis is crowded on both sides. Sentiment is getting more bullish exactly as positioning gets more fragile. That's usually not how cycles end well.
items.tweetB: If professional respondents keep pushing their 'AI inning' estimate earlier every six months, that's not a forecast, that's a moving goalpost. Worth tracking how many times this survey resets before someone admits the inning count was wrong from the start.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (paywalled beyond the survey teaser)

items.title: McKinsey: global investment patterns have 'dramatically diverged' — and there are 7 levers to fix it
items.idea: New MGI research (Kortis, Mischke, Bradley, Johansson, Singhal et al.) argues competitiveness has become the top item on the global economic agenda, framed around investment as both cause and effect — the report structures 10 detailed investment case studies and seven levers regions and companies can pull to 'level up.'
items.angle: Thin on specifics in the email itself, but the framing — competitiveness-as-investment-allocation — is the same lens useful for reading capex-heavy AI/energy stories in this window; worth pulling the underlying report for the specific levers and case studies.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's framing this week: competitiveness isn't culture or policy anymore, it's just 'where does capital actually go.' That's the same lens explaining why SK Hynix, Micron, and every AI infra name are suddenly capital-raising machines — investment has become the whole competitiveness story, everywhere.
items.tweetB: Ten investment case studies, seven levers, one thesis: regions and companies that don't actively fight for capital allocation lose the competitiveness race by default. Worth checking which of the 7 levers your portfolio's laggards are actually missing.

items.title: Cloudflare is about to run the first real test of 'making bots pay for the internet'
items.idea: x402 (a crypto micropayment rail for AI agents) saw June transactions settle roughly 2x above May, concentrated around one inference-routing seller (BlockRun). Amazon integrated the protocol at the AWS edge; Cloudflare — which sits in front of roughly 20% of the web — opened its Content Monetization Gateway waitlist July 1, letting any site charge AI bots per-request in stablecoins via x402. Cloudflare's own CEO flagged the catch: monetizing even a slice of its traffic needs millions of transactions per second, beyond what any chain he's tested can do.
items.angle: This is the actual mechanism by which 'AI is eating the web's ad economics' gets fixed or doesn't — Cloudflare's 20% market share makes this the highest-stakes real-world stress test of stablecoin micropayments to date, independent of any crypto-price narrative.
items.tweetA: Cloudflare sits in front of ~20% of the internet. On July 1st they opened the waitlist to charge AI bots per-request in stablecoins. This is the actual answer to 'how do publishers survive bots reading content with no ad clicks' — and it's happening on crypto rails, not inside a Google product.
items.tweetB: Cloudflare's own CEO said the quiet part: charging AI traffic at scale needs millions of transactions per second, and no blockchain he's tested can do it yet. The real 2026 crypto infra story isn't a new L1 — it's whether any chain can handle Cloudflare's bot traffic before someone builds a centralized alternative that doesn't need one.

items.title: Frontier AI models are so good at finding backdoors that 'Chinese hardware = spy risk' may stop making sense
items.idea: Eoyang argues Mythos-class models (Anthropic Mythos/Fable, GPT-5.6, GLM 5.2) are now powerful enough at vulnerability discovery that hiding a government backdoor in any hardware — Chinese, American, or European — becomes nearly impossible to sustain, since defenders can run the same models against their own stack and find it first. That undercuts the security rationale (distinct from the industrial-policy rationale) for banning Chinese EVs, drones, and routers.
items.angle: If this holds, it reframes years of 'national security' tech bans as really being industrial policy in a security costume — worth watching whether US policy actually adjusts, or the bans persist anyway once the stated rationale erodes (the tell that it was never really about security).
items.tweetA: If an AI model can find backdoors in Chinese routers, the same model can find backdoors in American ones. ChinaTalk's argument: 'Chinese hardware = spy risk' stops being a security argument once Mythos-class models can audit ANY hardware equally well. It becomes just industrial policy wearing a security badge.
items.tweetB: The tell to watch for: if the US keeps banning Chinese tech even after AI-assisted auditing removes the security justification, that confirms the bans were about protecting US manufacturers all along, not backdoors. Watch what DDTC and Commerce actually do here, not what they say.

items.source: The Market House / Macro Sessions (Juliette Declercq, JDI Research)
items.title: JDI's Declercq: the oil shock isn't why inflation is sticky — AI capex is
items.idea: Declercq says core+supercore inflation is running above 4% on a 6-month basis and will stay sticky; she pushes back on the consensus view that oil is the main driver, pointing to exploding import prices in plastics and chips, not just energy. She calls current AI impact 'super inflationary' (raising costs weighed against labor budgets) even though it may eventually turn disinflationary. She's bearish long-end bonds, sees the 10yr potentially hitting 5%.
items.angle: This directly contradicts the 'AI capex is deflationary in the long run' bull case circulating elsewhere — her claim that AI is inflationary RIGHT NOW (via component/chip/import costs) is the more falsifiable, near-term version of that debate, and it supports being short duration.
items.tweetA: Declercq's contrarian call: the Iran oil shock isn't what's making inflation sticky — AI capex is. Import prices in plastics and chips are exploding, not just energy. If she's right, the 'AI is disinflationary' consensus has the timing backwards: inflationary now, disinflationary maybe later. $TLT shorts make sense either way.
items.tweetB: 10-year at 5% isn't a tail-risk call anymore, it's JDI Research's actual bearish base case if data stays strong and the Fed keeps talking hawkish without delivering hikes. Worth pricing what an AI capex supercycle running alongside a rate shock does to every long-duration growth stock in the index.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (47-min video, first 16 min free)

items.source: The Market House / Macro Sessions (Juliette Declercq, JDI Research)
items.title: Trump can't afford higher gas before the midterms — and Iran knows it
items.idea: Declercq frames Iran's leverage as explicitly political: Trump 'cannot afford to have gas coming back higher ahead of the midterms,' so 'whatever Iran does he will fold into it' — creating incentive for Iran to escalate ('get more than what they've already got just because they can') in a possible 'mini crisis this summer.'
items.angle: This is a specific, falsifiable trade thesis on political-economy grounds rather than pure military analysis — if true, oil spikes get met with policy accommodation rather than resolve, changing the asymmetry of shorting oil into any escalation headline.
items.tweetA: Declercq's read on Iran leverage: it's not military, it's electoral. Trump needs gas prices down before the midterms more than he needs Iran's enriched uranium contained — so Iran has every incentive to test that weakness this summer. That's a specific, tradeable political constraint, not vague geopolitical risk.
items.tweetB: If the market is pricing 'Trump wins the standoff with Iran,' Declercq's framing says the opposite structurally makes more sense: the side that needs a calm outcome before an election is the side with less leverage, not more. Worth fading any oil-down move that's really just midterm optimism.

items.title: Nvidia's valuation just fell to a 7-year low — 'pre-AI boom' territory
items.idea: TheStreet's Thursday wrap notes Nvidia's valuation has fallen back to levels last seen before the AI boom began (a 7-year low on their measure), even as the Russell 2000 and Nasdaq both bounced 1.3-1.4% after the latest US-Iran exchange.
items.angle: A '7-year low' valuation framing on Nvidia deserves scrutiny — worth checking whether that's a forward-multiple compression story (bullish: cheaper AI growth) or an implied-growth-collapse story (bearish), since the same fact supports opposite trades depending on which it is.
items.tweetA: Nvidia's valuation is back to 'pre-AI-boom' levels by TheStreet's measure. Either that's the best entry point of the cycle because earnings caught up to the hype, or it's the market quietly pricing in that the hype was never going to be earned back. Same headline number, opposite trades — check which one it is before you act on it.
items.tweetB: A 7-year-low valuation on the stock most identified with the AI boom, in the same week AI infra capex chatter (Micron $250B, SK Hynix ADR) is hitting records, is a genuine divergence worth sitting with rather than explaining away in one tweet.

items.title: Meta's fourth pivot in a row: crypto, metaverse, neocloud, now always-on smart glasses
items.idea: Meta has cycled through a failed crypto/stablecoin push, billions burned on the metaverse, and an AI-turned-neocloud compute bet that has eaten company cash. Its latest bet, doubling down on smart glasses, comes despite explicit backlash over always-on privacy concerns.
items.angle: Four consecutive 'next big thing' pivots at a company still printing enormous Instagram/WhatsApp ad cash is a pattern worth tracking as a capital-discipline question, independent of whether any single bet (including this one) actually works.
items.tweetA: Meta has now tried crypto, the metaverse, and neocloud compute, and struck out or burned cash on all three. Now it's betting the company's 'next big thing' on always-on smart glasses despite explicit privacy backlash. Fourth pivot in a row — the ad business is funding an awful lot of expensive experiments.
items.tweetB: The through-line across Meta's crypto, metaverse, neocloud, and now smart-glasses bets isn't the technology, it's the pattern: identify a frontier, spend billions before product-market fit is proven, and let the ad cash machine absorb the loss. Worth asking when that stops being 'visionary' and starts being a governance problem.

items.title: The human-data-for-AI industry now has three $1B+ ARR companies you've never heard of
items.idea: SemiAnalysis reports Mercor, Surge, and Handshake — RL-environment/human-data providers — are each at $1B+ ARR, with newer entrants (Fleet, Mechanize, Afterquery) already near $100M ARR after barely a year in business. The piece frames RL environments (not raw data) as the actual scaling-law bottleneck now, flagging screen recordings of white-collar work as the next resource these companies are hunting for.
items.angle: This is the AI supply-chain layer nobody prices directly — if RL environment supply is the real bottleneck (per Anthropic's Sholto Douglas, quoted saying current algorithms are 'trivially worthwhile' to scale against white-collar-work TAM), these unlisted data companies are capturing value public AI-infra investors can't currently buy.
items.tweetA: Three companies you've never heard of — Mercor, Surge, Handshake — are each doing $1B+ ARR selling 'human data' for AI training. That's a bigger, more investable bottleneck right now than another GPU chip story, and there's no public way to buy it yet.
items.tweetB: Anthropic's Sholto Douglas, on record: even with zero further algorithm progress, current RL methods are 'trivially worthwhile' to scale against the entire TAM of white-collar salaries — IF you have enough of the right data. That's the whole AI-labor-displacement debate in one quote, from someone building the thing.

items.title: Meta's new datacenter network can't beat the speed of light — so it's designing around it
items.idea: Meta's Prometheus cluster uses a multi-layer fabric (BAG aggregation connecting up to 5 DSF or 7 NSF regions, then an L4 Inter-BAG hub delivering ~22 petabits/sec bi-directional). Latency within a single datacenter region is 1-10µs, but a site 100km away can't be reached in under 500µs due to pure light-propagation physics — forcing Meta to run pretraining synchronously within one region while spreading RL workloads asynchronously across the globe. Competitors are reportedly pushing 'scale-across' architectures spanning up to 2,000km between campuses.
items.angle: This is the physical constraint that ultimately caps how 'distributed' a frontier training run can be — worth knowing as a hard limit when reading claims about infinitely scalable global AI compute clusters.
items.tweetA: 500 microseconds. That's the physical floor, not an engineering choice, for round-tripping data between datacenters 100km apart, because light can't go faster. Meta's entire Prometheus network design (22 petabits/sec at L4) exists specifically to work around a limit nobody can code their way out of.
items.tweetB: Everyone talks about AI compute scaling like it's infinite. The real constraint in Meta's Prometheus cluster: pretraining has to stay synchronous within a single ~100km region, RL can go global and async. The next bottleneck in AI isn't chips, it's the speed of light between datacenters 2,000km apart.

items.title: Meta's $14.3B superteam just shipped a model that's merely 'on par' with rivals — and that might be the point
items.idea: After the Llama 4 disaster, Meta spent $14.3B to acquire Alexandr Wang (Scale AI) plus $1B+ on Nat Friedman/Daniel Gross's fund, poached 14+ researchers (mostly ex-OpenAI, some ex-Anthropic/Google), and pays $1B+ packages to top researchers. Their first public model, Muse Spark 1.1, landed roughly on par with Opus 4.6/GLM 5.2 — a lateral move, not SOTA — with engineers noting it ignores warnings and misuses tools. SemiAnalysis's own team isn't moving any internal token volume to it yet, calling this 'step 1' of a rebuild.
items.angle: The interesting trade isn't 'is Muse Spark good' (it isn't, yet) — it's that $15B+ in acquisition/comp spend bought Meta a lateral model, either wildly expensive tuition or the necessary reset before a real jump; SemiAnalysis's own zero-token-migration usage data is the more honest signal than the model's benchmark scores.
items.tweetA: Meta spent $14.3B just to acquire the guy (Scale AI's Alexandr Wang), then $1B+ pay packages on top for researchers, and the resulting model — Muse Spark 1.1 — lands roughly even with Opus 4.6. Not better. Even. SemiAnalysis's own engineers aren't routing a single token to it yet. That's the most expensive 'lateral move' in tech history.
items.tweetB: The tell isn't Muse Spark's benchmark score, it's that SemiAnalysis — who tests everything — isn't moving any of their own internal AI usage to Meta's new model. When the AI research shop testing your product won't eat its own dog food, that's worth more than any leaderboard rank.

items.source: WealthVest Macro (Drew & Tim, guest Manoj Pradhan)
items.title: Talking Heads' Pradhan on Baumol's cost disease, fiscal dominance, and the Fed's bind
items.idea: WealthVest's podcast this week features Manoj Pradhan (Talking Heads Macroeconomics) discussing demographics, Baumol's cost disease (services costs structurally rising faster than goods), fiscal dominance (debt levels forcing monetary policy to serve fiscal needs), and the resulting bind facing the Fed between high debt and secular inflation.
items.angle: 'Fiscal dominance' as a framing is becoming more mainstream across podcast/newsletter circuits — worth tracking as a meme because it directly informs the bear-bond thesis (Declercq's 10yr-at-5% call above) and any Fed-independence debate.
items.tweetA: 'Fiscal dominance' keeps showing up in more macro conversations every month — this week it's Manoj Pradhan on WealthVest. The idea: debt levels get so large that monetary policy stops being about inflation and starts being forced to serve the government's financing needs. That's a regime change, not a cycle.
items.tweetB: Baumol's cost disease used to be a niche econ-textbook term. Now it's showing up alongside 'fiscal dominance' in the same breath on mainstream macro podcasts. When two wonky frameworks start traveling together, it usually means practitioners are groping for a new explanation because the old cyclical playbook stopped working.
